{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01ArCAoSe9T3ybwp3eS2P2XJ","type":"tool_result","content":"     1→import posixpath\n     2→from collections import defaultdict\n     3→\n     4→from django.utils.safestring import mark_safe\n     5→\n     6→from .base import Node, Template, TemplateSyntaxError, TextNode, Variable, token_kwargs\n     7→from .library import Library\n     8→\n     9→register = Library()\n    10→\n    11→BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY = \"block_context\"\n    12→\n    13→\n    14→class BlockContext:\n    15→    def __init__(self):\n    16→        # Dictionary of FIFO queues.\n    17→        self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n    18→\n    19→    def __repr__(self):\n    20→        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n    21→\n    22→    def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n    23→        for name, block in blocks.items():\n    24→            self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n    25→\n    26→    def pop(self, name):\n    27→        try:\n    28→            return self.blocks[name].pop()\n    29→        except IndexError:\n    30→            return None\n    31→\n    32→    def push(self, name, block):\n    33→        self.blocks[name].append(block)\n    34→\n    35→    def get_block(self, name):\n    36→        try:\n    37→            return self.blocks[name][-1]\n    38→        except IndexError:\n    39→            return None\n    40→\n    41→\n    42→class BlockNode(Node):\n    43→    def __init__(self, name, nodelist, parent=None):\n    44→        self.name = name\n    45→        self.nodelist = nodelist\n    46→        self.parent = parent\n    47→\n    48→    def __repr__(self):\n    49→        return \"<Block Node: %s. Contents: %r>\" % (self.name, self.nodelist)\n    50→\n    51→    def render(self, context):\n    52→        block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\n    53→        with context.push():\n    54→            if block_context is None:\n    55→                context[\"block\"] = self\n    56→                result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n    57→            else:\n    58→                push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n    59→                if block is None:\n    60→                    block = self\n    61→                # Create new block so we can store context without\n    62→                # thread-safety issues.\n    63→                block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n    64→                block.context = context\n    65→                context[\"block\"] = block\n    66→                result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n    67→                if push is not None:\n    68→                    block_context.push(self.name, push)\n    69→        return result\n    70→\n    71→    def super(self):\n    72→        if not hasattr(self, \"context\"):\n    73→            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n    74→                \"'%s' object has no attribute 'context'. Did you use \"\n    75→                \"{{ block.super }} in a base template?\" % self.__class__.__name__\n    76→            )\n    77→        render_context = self.context.render_context\n    78→        if (\n    79→            BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY in render_context\n    80→            and render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY].get_block(self.name) is not None\n    81→        ):\n    82→            return mark_safe(self.render(self.context))\n    83→        return \"\"\n    84→\n    85→\n    86→class ExtendsNode(Node):\n    87→    must_be_first = True\n    88→    context_key = \"extends_context\"\n    89→\n    90→    def __init__(self, nodelist, parent_name, template_dirs=None):\n    91→        self.nodelist = nodelist\n    92→        self.parent_name = parent_name\n    93→        self.template_dirs = template_dirs\n    94→        self.blocks = {n.name: n for n in nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)}\n    95→\n    96→    def __repr__(self):\n    97→        return \"<%s: extends %s>\" % (self.__class__.__name__, self.parent_name.token)\n    98→\n    99→    def find_template(self, template_name, context):\n   100→        \"\"\"\n   101→        This is a wrapper around engine.find_template(). A history is kept in\n   102→        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n   103→        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n   104→        without extending the same template twice.\n   105→        \"\"\"\n   106→        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n   107→            self.context_key,\n   108→            [self.origin],\n   109→        )\n   110→        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n   111→            template_name,\n   112→            skip=history,\n   113→        )\n   114→        history.append(origin)\n   115→        return template\n   116→\n   117→    def get_parent(self, context):\n   118→        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n   119→        if not parent:\n   120→            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n   121→            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n   122→                error_msg += (\n   123→                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n   124→                )\n   125→            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n   126→        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n   127→            # parent is a django.template.Template\n   128→            return parent\n   129→        if isinstance(getattr(parent, \"template\", None), Template):\n   130→            # parent is a django.template.backends.django.Template\n   131→            return parent.template\n   132→        return self.find_template(parent, context)\n   133→\n   134→    def render(self, context):\n   135→        compiled_parent = self.get_parent(context)\n   136→\n   137→        if BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY not in context.render_context:\n   138→            context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY] = BlockContext()\n   139→        block_context = context.render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY]\n   140→\n   141→        # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\n   142→        block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n   143→\n   144→        # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\n   145→        # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n   146→        for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n   147→            # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\n   148→            if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n   149→                if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n   150→                    blocks = {\n   151→                        n.name: n\n   152→                        for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n   153→                    }\n   154→                    block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n   155→                break\n   156→\n   157→        # Call Template._render explicitly so the parser context stays\n   158→        # the same.\n   159→        with context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):\n   160→            return compiled_parent._render(context)\n   161→\n   162→\n   163→class IncludeNode(Node):\n   164→    context_key = \"__include_context\"\n   165→\n   166→    def __init__(\n   167→        self, template, *args, extra_context=None, isolated_context=False, **kwargs\n   168→    ):\n   169→        self.template = template\n   170→        self.extra_context = extra_context or {}\n   171→        self.isolated_context = isolated_context\n   172→        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\n   173→\n   174→    def __repr__(self):\n   175→        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: template={self.template!r}>\"\n   176→\n   177→    def render(self, context):\n   178→        \"\"\"\n   179→        Render the specified template and context. Cache the template object\n   180→        in render_context to avoid reparsing and loading when used in a for\n   181→        loop.\n   182→        \"\"\"\n   183→        template = self.template.resolve(context)\n   184→        # Does this quack like a Template?\n   185→        if not callable(getattr(template, \"render\", None)):\n   186→            # If not, try the cache and select_template().\n   187→            template_name = template or ()\n   188→            if isinstance(template_name, str):\n   189→                template_name = (\n   190→                    construct_relative_path(\n   191→                        self.origin.template_name,\n   192→                        template_name,\n   193→                    ),\n   194→                )\n   195→            else:\n   196→                template_name = tuple(template_name)\n   197→            cache = context.render_context.dicts[0].setdefault(self, {})\n   198→            template = cache.get(template_name)\n   199→            if template is None:\n   200→                template = context.template.engine.select_template(template_name)\n   201→                cache[template_name] = template\n   202→        # Use the base.Template of a backends.django.Template.\n   203→        elif hasattr(template, \"template\"):\n   204→            template = template.template\n   205→        values = {\n   206→            name: var.resolve(context) for name, var in self.extra_context.items()\n   207→        }\n   208→        if self.isolated_context:\n   209→            return template.render(context.new(values))\n   210→        with context.push(**values):\n   211→            return template.render(context)\n   212→\n   213→\n   214→@register.tag(\"block\")\n   215→def do_block(parser, token):\n   216→    \"\"\"\n   217→    Define a block that can be overridden by child templates.\n   218→    \"\"\"\n   219→    # token.split_contents() isn't useful here because this tag doesn't accept\n   220→    # variable as arguments.\n   221→    bits = token.contents.split()\n   222→    if len(bits) != 2:\n   223→        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' tag takes only one argument\" % bits[0])\n   224→    block_name = bits[1]\n   225→    # Keep track of the names of BlockNodes found in this template, so we can\n   226→    # check for duplication.\n   227→    try:\n   228→        if block_name in parser.__loaded_blocks:\n   229→            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n   230→                \"'%s' tag with name '%s' appears more than once\" % (bits[0], block_name)\n   231→            )\n   232→        parser.__loaded_blocks.append(block_name)\n   233→    except AttributeError:  # parser.__loaded_blocks isn't a list yet\n   234→        parser.__loaded_blocks = [block_name]\n   235→    nodelist = parser.parse((\"endblock\",))\n   236→\n   237→    # This check is kept for backwards-compatibility. See #3100.\n   238→    endblock = parser.next_token()\n   239→    acceptable_endblocks = (\"endblock\", \"endblock %s\" % block_name)\n   240→    if endblock.contents not in acceptable_endblocks:\n   241→        parser.invalid_block_tag(endblock, \"endblock\", acceptable_endblocks)\n   242→\n   243→    return BlockNode(block_name, nodelist)\n   244→\n   245→\n   246→def construct_relative_path(\n   247→    current_template_name,\n   248→    relative_name,\n   249→    allow_recursion=False,\n   250→):\n   251→    \"\"\"\n   252→    Convert a relative path (starting with './' or '../') to the full template\n   253→    name based on the current_template_name.\n   254→    \"\"\"\n   255→    new_name = relative_name.strip(\"'\\\"\")\n   256→    if not new_name.startswith((\"./\", \"../\")):\n   257→        # relative_name is a variable or a literal that doesn't contain a\n   258→        # relative path.\n   259→        return relative_name\n   260→\n   261→    if current_template_name is None:\n   262→        # Unknown origin (e.g. Template('...').render(Context({...})).\n   263→        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n   264→            f\"The relative path {relative_name} cannot be evaluated due to \"\n   265→            \"an unknown template origin.\"\n   266→        )\n   267→\n   268→    new_name = posixpath.normpath(\n   269→        posixpath.join(\n   270→            posixpath.dirname(current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\")),\n   271→            new_name,\n   272→        )\n   273→    )\n   274→    if new_name.startswith(\"../\"):\n   275→        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n   276→            \"The relative path '%s' points outside the file hierarchy that \"\n   277→            \"template '%s' is in.\" % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n   278→        )\n   279→    if not allow_recursion and current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\") == new_name:\n   280→        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n   281→            \"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \"\n   282→            \"same template in which the tag appears.\"\n   283→            % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n   284→        )\n   285→    has_quotes = (\n   286→        relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\n   287→    )\n   288→    return f'\"{new_name}\"' if has_quotes else new_name\n   289→\n   290→\n   291→@register.tag(\"extends\")\n   292→def do_extends(parser, token):\n   293→    \"\"\"\n   294→    Signal that this template extends a parent template.\n   295→\n   296→    This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \"base\" %}`` (with quotes)\n   297→    uses the literal value \"base\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\n   298→    or ``{% extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n   299→    name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n   300→    the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a which the tag appears.\"\n            % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    has_quotes = (\n        relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == The core Node\n    18→types are TextNode, VariableNode, IfNode and ForNode, but plugin modules can\n    19→define their own custom node types.\n    20→\n    21→Each Node has a render() method, which takes a Context and returns a string of\n    22→the rendered node. For example, the render() method of a Variable Node returns\n    23→the variable's value as a string. The render() method of a ForNode returns the\n    24→rendered output of whatever was inside the loop, recursively.\n    25→\n    26→The Template class is a convenient wrapper that takes care of template\n    27→compilation and rendering.\n    28→\n    29→Usage:\n    30→\n    31→The only thing you should ever use directly in this file is the Template class.\n    32→Create a compiled template object with a template_string, then call render()\n    33→with a context. The core Node\ntypes are TextNode, VariableNode, IfNode and ForNode, but plugin modules can\ndefine their own custom node types.\n\nEach Node has a render() method, which takes a Context and returns a string of\nthe rendered node. For example, the render() method of a Variable Node returns\nthe variable's value as a string. The render() method of a ForNode returns the\nrendered output of whatever was inside the loop, recursively.\n\nThe Template class is a convenient wrapper that takes care of template\ncompilation and rendering.\n\nUsage:\n\nThe only thing you should ever use directly in this file is the Template class.\nCreate a compiled template object with a template_string, then call render()\nwith a context. In the compilation stage, the TemplateSyntaxError exception\nwill be raised if the template doesn't have proper syntax.\n\nSample code:\n\n>>> from django import template\n>>> s = '<html>{% if test %}<h1>{{ varvalue }}</h1>{% endif %}</html>'\n>>> t = template.Template(s)\n\n(t is now a compiled template, and its render() method can be called multiple\ntimes with multiple contexts)\n\n>>> c = template.Context({'test':True, 'varvalue': 'Hello'})\n>>> t.render(c)\n'<html><h1>Hello</h1></html>'\n>>> c = template.Context({'test':False, 'varvalue': 'Hello'})\n>>> t.render(c)\n'<html></html>'\n\"\"\"\n\nimport inspect\nimport logging\nimport re\nimport warnings\nfrom enum import Enum\n\nfrom django.template.context import BaseContext\nfrom django.utils.deprecation import django_file_prefixes\nfrom django.utils.formats import localize\nfrom django.utils.html import conditional_escape\nfrom django.utils.inspect import lazy_annotations\nfrom django.utils.regex_helper import _lazy_re_compile\nfrom django.utils.safestring import SafeData, SafeString, mark_safe\nfrom django.utils.text import get_text_list, smart_split, unescape_string_literal\nfrom django.utils.timezone import template_localtime\nfrom django.utils.translation import gettext_lazy, pgettext_lazy\n\nfrom .exceptions import TemplateSyntaxError\n\n# template syntax constants\nFILTER_SEPARATOR = \"|\"\nFILTER_ARGUMENT_SEPARATOR = \":\"\nVARIABLE_ATTRIBUTE_SEPARATOR = \".\"\nBLOCK_TAG_START = \"{%\"\nBLOCK_TAG_END = \"%}\"\nVARIABLE_TAG_START = \"{{\"\nVARIABLE_TAG_END = \"}}\"\nCOMMENT_TAG_START = \"{#\"\nCOMMENT_TAG_END = \"#}\"\nSINGLE_BRACE_START = \"{\"\nSINGLE_BRACE_END = \"}\"\n\n# what to report as the origin for templates that come from non-loader sources\n# (e.g. strings)\nUNKNOWN_SOURCE = \"<unknown source>\"\n\n# Match BLOCK_TAG_*, VARIABLE_TAG_*, and COMMENT_TAG_* tags and capture the\n# entire tag, including start/end delimiters. Using re.compile() is faster\n# than instantiating SimpleLazyObject with _lazy_re_compile().\ntag_re = re.compile(r\"({%.*?%}|{{.*?}}|{#.*?#})\")\n\nlogger = logging.getLogger(\"django.template\")\n\n\nclass TokenType(Enum):\n    TEXT = 0\n    VAR = 1\n    BLOCK = 2","numLines":100,"startLine":1,"totalLines":1208}}}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01L2ZCfGyZfCn6hEbZsHnoCv","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"I see. Let me try a different approach. Rather than trying to run tests, let me just analyze the code to understand the issue and create a fix. Then I'll validate it by reading through the test cases manually.\n\nLet me go back to understanding the template inheritance logic. Looking at the loader_tags.py code again:\n\nIn `ExtendsNode.render()`:\n1. It gets the parent template\n2. Creates/gets a `BlockContext`\n3. Calls `block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)` to add the child's blocks\n4. If the parent doesn't have an extends node (it's the root), it also adds the parent's blocks\n5. Renders the parent template\n\nThe issue is likely in how nested blocks are handled. When a block contains another block:\n- The parent block's content includes the child block\n- The child block is registered in the `BlockContext`\n- When rendered, the parent block should render its content, which includes the child block\n\nLet me trace through what happens with 3 levels:\n\nLevel 1: base.html\n```\n{% block outer %}outer_base{% endblock %}\n```\n\nLevel 2: middle.html extends base.html\n```\n{% extends \"base.html\" %}\n{% block outer %}outer_middle {% block inner %}inner_middle{% endblock %}{% endblock %}\n```\n\nLevel 3: child.html extends middle.html\n```\n{% extends \"middle.html\" %}\n{% block inner %}inner_child{% endblock %}\n```\n\nWhen rendering child.html:\n1. ExtendsNode for \"middle.html\" is processed\n2. It collects blocks from child.html: {inner -> BlockNode}\n3. It adds these to block_context: block_context.add_blocks({inner -> BlockNode})\n4. It renders middle.html\n\nWhen rendering middle.html:\n1. ExtendsNode for \"base.html\" is processed\n2. It collects blocks from middle.html: {outer -> BlockNode(contains inner BlockNode), inner -> BlockNode}\n\n\n3. It adds these to block_context\n\nThe `get_nodes_by_type` method retrieves all BlockNodes from the nodelist, including nested ones. So when parsing middle.html, both \"outer\" and \"inner\" blocks should be collected. The problem might stem from the order in which blocks are registered in the block_context—when a parent block contains a child block, the registration sequence could affect which version gets rendered.\n\nLooking at how `add_blocks()` inserts at the beginning of the list and `pop()` retrieves from the end, there's a LIFO pattern at play. When middle.html's blocks are added first followed by child.html's blocks, the child's \"inner\" block should override the parent's version when popped. But the issue is that the blocks are stored as lists, and the pop mechanism works from the end of the list. Wait, let me reconsider this. When `nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)` is called, it recursively retrieves all BlockNodes from the entire nodelist, including those nested within other blocks. So for middle.html with its outer block containing an inner block, both BlockNode(\"outer\") and BlockNode(\"inner\") would be captured by that recursive search. This means the block_context dictionary gets populated with both the parent and child blocks from the same template level.\n\nDuring the three-level inheritance chain (child → middle → base), each ExtendsNode collects its blocks and adds them to the block_context queue. When base.html finally renders and encounters a block tag, it pulls from this accumulated context, which now contains block definitions from all three templates stacked in order.\n\nThe key mechanism is how blocks get queued: middle.html's blocks are inserted at position 0, so when inner gets popped, it retrieves the most recently added override (middle_inner) first, followed by child_inner. This ensures the correct precedence—the closest parent's block definition takes priority over the child's.\n\nLooking at the ExtendsNode.render() method around lines 146-155, there's logic to handle root templates: if a parent doesn't have its own extends node, its block nodes get added to the block context directly. This ensures all blocks in the inheritance chain are properly registered and available for rendering.\n\nThe issue becomes apparent with three-level inheritance. When child.html's ExtendsNode processes, it collects {inner} and adds these to block_context, then renders middle.html. When middle.html's ExtendsNode subsequently processes, it collects {outer, inner} from its own template and adds them to the context. The problem is that middle.html's inner block overwrites the child's inner block that was already registered.\n\nThe block queue ends up as [middle_inner, child_inner], and since pop() removes from the end of the list, we retrieve child_inner first—which seems correct. But the real issue is that middle.html's blocks shouldn't be added to the context at all when it's being rendered as a parent; only the child's blocks should override the parent's defaults. in middle.html, it's being treated as a separate top-level block. When the outer block renders, it might be processing before the inner block is properly removed from the stack.\n\nLooking at BlockNode.render(), when a block is popped from block_context, it creates a new instance and stores the context. The issue could be that nested blocks defined within parent blocks aren't being handled correctly—the inner block might still be in the context when outer tries to render, causing it to use the wrong version or render multiple times. child.html -> middle.html -> base.html:\n\nInitial block_context from child.html: {inner -> [child_inner]}\n\nWhen middle.html's ExtendsNode.render() is called:\n1. Collects blocks: {outer, inner}\n2. Adds to block_context: block_context.add_blocks({outer -> middle_outer, inner -> middle_inner})\n   - After this: {outer -> [middle_outer], inner -> [middle_inner, child_inner]}\n3. Renders base.html\n\nWhen base.html renders:\n1. Encounters block \"outer\"\n2. BlockNode(\"outer\").render() is called:\n   - push = block_context.pop(\"outer\") -> Returns middle_outer\n   - Creates a new block from middle_outer\n   - Renders block.nodelist (which is the content of middle_outer)\n3. Encounters block \"inner\"\n4. BlockNode(\"inner\").render() is called:\n   - push = block_context.pop(\"inner\") -> Returns middle_inner\n   - Renders middle_inner's content, which may contain another block tag\n   - If middle_inner has a block \"inner\" inside it, that BlockNode.render() calls pop(\"inner\") again -> Returns child_inner\n   - Renders child_inner's content\n   - Pushes child_inner back onto the queue\n   - Returns to rendering middle_inner\n   - Pushes middle_inner back onto the queue\n\nThe issue becomes clear when examining the expected behavior: the innermost template's block should completely replace the middle template's block, not render both. The problem is that middle_inner gets rendered even though child_inner should take precedence.\n\nLooking at the block structure more carefully, when middle.html is parsed, it creates nested BlockNodes - one for \"outer\" containing a TextNode and another BlockNode for \"inner\". The critical question is whether `get_nodes_by_type()` recursively searches inside other BlockNodes, or if it only finds top-level blocks. This could cause issues if those nested blocks haven't been properly set up for rendering yet.\n\nBut wait, that's not directly the cause of the \"leaking through\" issue described.\n\nLet me re-think the issue. The report says the content from the middle template leaks through. Let me re-examine what happens:\n\nInitial state: child.html wants to override the \"inner\" block\n\nStep 1: child.html's ExtendsNode.render()\n- Collects blocks from child.html: {inner}\n- Adds to block_context: blocks[\"inner\"] = [child_inner]\n- Renders middle.html (the parent)\n\nStep 2: middle.html's ExtendsNode.render()\n- Collects blocks from middle.html: {outer, inner}\n- Adds to block_context:\n  - blocks[\"outer\"] = [middle_outer]\n  - blocks[\"inner\"].insert(0, middle_inner) -> [middle_inner, child_inner]\n- Checks if base.html extends:\n  - It doesn't, so adds base.html's blocks: {outer}\n  - blocks[\"outer\"].insert(0, base_outer) -> [base_outer, middle_outer]\n- Renders base.html\n\nStep 3: Render base.html\n- Encounters block \"outer\"\n- pop(\"outer\") removes from the end: middle_outer (list becomes [base_outer])\n\n\n- Creates new BlockNode from middle_outer\n- Renders middle_outer's nodelist, which contains the inner BlockNode\n\nStep 4: Render the nested inner BlockNode\n- This is where the problem might be!\n- pop(\"inner\") removes from the end: child_inner\n- But wait, let me check if the inner BlockNode being rendered is the right one...\n\nActually, I think I see the issue now! NO WAIT, there's only one inner block definition in middle.html\n\nHmm, let me re-examine the actual block collection. When `get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)` is called on middle.html, it recursively finds all BlockNodes. So it finds both the outer block AND the inner block that's nested inside it. The inner block from middle.html gets added to the block_context, but then when rendering, the nested inner BlockNode from middle_outer's nodelist is rendered directly without checking if there's an override in the context. we render middle_outer's nodelist, we hit the BlockNode(\"inner\") nested inside outer. At that point, block_context.blocks[\"inner\"] contains [middle_inner, child_inner], so popping gives us child_inner—exactly what we want.\n\nThe key insight is that when middle.html's ExtendsNode calls get_nodes_by_type() to populate self.blocks, it retrieves the nested BlockNode(\"inner\") from inside outer's nodelist. This same BlockNode instance appears in both places: as a child of outer in the nodelist, and as the value for blocks[\"inner\"]. So when rendering encounters that BlockNode during nodelist traversal, it's working with the identical object whose name gets looked up in the block context. BlockNode(\"inner\", [TextNode(\"inner_child\")])})\n\nThe child's block gets added to the queue, so blocks[\"inner\"] now contains [middle_inner_nested, child_inner]. When we pop and render child_inner, we get the correct nodelist with [TextNode(\"inner_child\")].\n\nLooking at the ExtendsNode.render() sequence: it first adds blocks from the current template to the context, then processes the parent's nodelist to find and add any root-level blocks. The ExtendsNode must be the first node in the parent's nodelist for this to work correctly. blocks[\"outer\"] gets both base_outer and middle_outer, while blocks[\"inner\"] remains [middle_inner_nested] since base.html doesn't define that block. When child adds its blocks, add_blocks inserts at position 0, making blocks[\"inner\"] = [child_inner, middle_inner_nested]. But popping from the end retrieves middle_inner_nested instead of child_inner—that's the actual problem. The root cause is how blocks are being inserted and retrieved from the stack. if base.html extends: NO\n   - Add base.html's blocks:\n     - blocks[\"outer\"].insert(0, base_outer) -> [base_outer, middle_outer]\n     - (base.html doesn't have \"inner\")\n   - Render base.html\n\n3. Render base.html\n   - Encounter block \"outer\"\n   - pop(\"outer\") -> middle_outer\n   - Render middle_outer's nodelist\n\n4. We pop from the END, so we get child_inner!\n\nUnless...OH! Maybe the issue is that after we render and push back\n\nThe stack order looks right when I trace through—popping from the end gives us the child's block first, which is what we want. But wait, there might be something happening after rendering and restoring blocks that's causing the problem.\n\nLooking at the BlockNode.render() implementation, after popping a block and rendering it, if there was a previous block in the stack, it gets pushed back onto the end. This means the order could shift—what was [middle_inner_nested, child_inner] before the pop becomes [middle_inner_nested] after popping child_inner, then [middle_inner_nested, child_inner] again after pushing it back. The append operation adds to the end, so the restoration might not preserve the original ordering we need.\n\nBut actually, tracing through the list operations more carefully: popping from [\"middle_inner_nested\", \"child_inner\"] removes and returns \"child_inner\", leaving [\"middle_inner_nested\"]. Then appending child_inner back gives [\"middle_inner_nested\", \"child_inner\"] again. So the mechanics check out.\n\nThe real problem might be in add_blocks—it always inserts at position 0, even when a block with that name already exists. This could cause duplicate blocks to accumulate in the stack rather than replacing existing ones.\n\nBut the issue description specifically says the middle template's content is leaking through instead of the base template's, which suggests the problem isn't about base template overrides. Looking at the three-level inheritance case where child.html overrides a nested block inside another overridden block, the middle template's content is appearing when it shouldn't. The block stack ordering during rendering must be causing the middle template's version to be found before the child template's version. .get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode) recursively collects all BlockNode instances from the nodelist, returning both the outer block and any nested blocks within it.\n\nFor middle.html, this means blocks[\"inner\"] initially points to the nested BlockNode(\"inner\") instance containing \"inner_middle\". When child.html's blocks are added first, blocks[\"inner\"] becomes [child_inner]. Then when middle.html's ExtendsNode.render() executes, it overwrites this with its own nested block instance, causing the wrong content to be rendered.\n\nThe real problem might be that the same BlockNode(\"inner\") instance from middle.html is being referenced in multiple places simultaneously—both in the blocks dictionary and potentially elsewhere in the template structure.\n\nWhen rendering the \"outer\" block, a new BlockNode instance gets created with the same nodelist, but the original BlockNode(\"inner\") within that nodelist remains unchanged. This means we're encountering and potentially rendering the same BlockNode instance multiple times during the rendering process, which could lead to unexpected behavior or state issues.\n\nThe logic for creating new instances during rendering appears sound—each block creates a fresh instance while preserving the nodelist structure. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01GGYgviuTXTf9E5nnGnTtgG","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"Good, Python 3.13 is available. Now let me try to install the minimal dependencies needed. But first, let me check if I can run a simpler test. Let me look at what the actual code flow should be and try to trace through it manually by reading the code.\n\nActually, I think I found the issue! Let me re-examine the ExtendsNode.render() code once more, paying close attention to WHEN the base template's blocks are added.\n\nLooking at lines 146-155:\n```python\nfor node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n    # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\n    if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n        if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n            blocks = {\n                n.name: n\n                for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n            }\n            block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n        break\n```\n\nThis loop checks the parent's nodelist. If the first non-text node is an ExtendsNode, it breaks without adding the parent's blocks. If it's NOT an ExtendsNode (meaning the parent is a root template), it adds the parent's blocks.\n\nNow here's the key: when middle.html's ExtendsNode.render() is called, it checks the parent's (base.html's) nodelist. base.html doesn't have an ExtendsNode, so its blocks are added.\n\nBut what blocks are found in base.html? The code calls:\n```python\nblocks = {\n    n.name: n\n    for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n}\n```\n\ncompiled_parent is base.html, so this finds all BlockNodes in base.html. But base.html only has one block: \"outer\". So blocks = {\"outer\": base_outer}.\n\nThen these are added to block_context:\n```python\nblock_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n\n\n```\n\nSo block_context.blocks[\"outer\"] becomes [base_outer] (or [base_outer, middle_outer] if middle's was already added). The sequence is: middle's blocks get added first with blocks[\"outer\"] = [middle_outer] and blocks[\"inner\"] = [middle_inner], then base's blocks are added so blocks[\"outer\"] = [base_outer, middle_outer]. When base.html renders and pops(\"outer\"), it retrieves middle_outer from the end of the stack, then renders its content which includes the inner block.\n\nWhen that inner block pops, block_context.blocks[\"inner\"] should have [middle_inner, child_inner], so we get child_inner. But there's something I'm missing about how blocks are collected. When middle's ExtendsNode calls get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode) on the parent's nodelist, it only finds top-level blocks like \"outer\" from base.html. The real problem might be that we're not recursively discovering nested blocks that exist deeper in the template structure.\n\n```\n\npython\nblocks = {\n    n.name: n\n    for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n}\n```\n\nThis approach only captures blocks at the surface level. Since base.html has no nested blocks anyway, that's not the issue here. The problem seems to be about the order in which blocks get added when middle.html renders—it's combining its own blocks with the parent's blocks simultaneously, which might be throwing off the expected sequence. Let me revert and think about this more carefully.\n\nThe issue is that we want the child's blocks to override the parent's blocks. With add_blocks() doing insert(0, block), adding to the front means the most recently added blocks are at the front.\n\nSo if we want the child's blocks to override (be popped first), we should add the child's blocks LAST (after the parent's blocks).\n\nBut that's what the original code was doing! It adds the child's blocks first (line 142), then the parent's blocks (line 154). This means:\n- Parent's blocks are at position 0 (front)\n- Child's blocks are further back in the queue\n\nWhen we pop(), we get from the end, so we get the child's blocks. That's correct!\n\nSo the original order was actually correct!\n\nLet me revert my change and think about this differently.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":1660,"cache_read_input_tokens":81566,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":1660},"output_tokens":2,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"5363e7cb-e5dd-47f7-a7a1-25f2b067b5b3","uuid":"4073ec7d-0641-4bb8-9797-9836f6437e36"}

Python's list.pop() without arguments removes and returns the LAST element (index -1). But my analysis shows we should be getting the right one. Unless the blocks aren't being added at the correct level in the first place? Then when middle.html's ExtendsNode.render() executes, it also calls add_blocks() with its own blocks.\n\nblocks[\"outer\"] = [middle_outer] and blocks[\"inner\"] = [middle_inner, child_inner]. For a popped block, the nodelist comes from the popped block, so if we pop child_inner and create a new BlockNode(\"inner\", child_inner.nodelist), we're rendering child_inner.nodelist which should contain \"child_inner\". When we create the dict with a comprehension, the order matches what get_nodes_by_type() returns. For middle.html, that would be [outer, inner], so outer gets processed first, then inner. Each key should be independent, unless there's something about processing one block that affects how the next one is handled.\n\nI'm wondering if the lists for different block names could somehow be referencing the same underlying data structure. That seems unlikely given they're separate keys in the defaultdict, but it's worth considering. If outer comes before inner in that list, then when add_blocks() processes them, outer gets registered first, which could impact how the inheritance chain resolves.\n\nWhen iterating through the dict, middle_outer inserts into blocks[\"outer\"] first, then middle_inner inserts into blocks[\"inner\"]. When middle.html gets parsed, the ExtendsNode finds all BlockNode instances in its nodelist using get_nodes_by_type(), which recursively searches through the entire tree. This means it discovers both the outer block and the inner block that's nested within it. The blocks dictionary ends up with both entries, but the inner block is still physically nested inside the outer block's content. Dictionary of FIFO queues.\n        self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n\n    def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n        for name, block in blocks.items():\n            self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n\n    def pop(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name].pop()\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n    def push(self, name, block):\n        self.blocks[name].append(block)\n\n    def get_block(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name][-1]\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n\nclass BlockNode(Node):\n    def __init__(self, name, nodelist, parent=None):\n        self.name = name\n        self.nodelist = nodelist\n        self.parent = parent\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return \"<Block Node: %s. A history is kept in\n        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n        without extending the same template twice.\n        \"\"\"\n        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n            self.context_key,\n            [self.origin],\n        )\n        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n            template_name,\n            skip=history,\n        )\n        history.append(origin)\n        return template\n\n    def get_parent(self, context):\n        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n        if not parent:\n            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n                error_msg += (\n                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n                )\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n            # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\n        block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n\n        # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\n        # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n        for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n            # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\n            if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n                if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n                    blocks = {\n                        n.name: n\n                        for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n                    }\n                    block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n                break\n\n        # Call Template._render explicitly so the parser context stays\n        # the same.\n        with context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):\n            return compiled_parent._render(context)\n\n\nclass IncludeNode(Node):\n    context_key = \"__include_context\"\n\n    def __init__(\n        self, template, *args, extra_context=None, isolated_context=False, **kwargs\n    ):\n        self.template = template\n        self.extra_context = extra_context or {}\n        self.isolated_context = isolated_context\n        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: template={self.template!r}>\"\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        \"\"\"\n        Render the specified template and context. Cache the template object\n        in render_context to avoid reparsing and loading when used in a for\n        loop.\n        \"\"\"\n        template = self.template.resolve(context)\n        # Does this quack like a Template?\n        if not callable(getattr(template, \"render\", None)):\n            # If not, try the cache and select_template().\n            template_name = template or ()\n            if isinstance(template_name, str):\n                template_name = (\n                    construct_relative_path(\n                        self.origin.template_name,\n                        template_name,\n                    ),\n                )\n            else:\n                template_name = tuple(template_name)\n            cache = context.render_context.dicts[0].setdefault(self, {})\n            template = cache.get(template_name)\n            if template is None:\n                template = context.template.engine.select_template(template_name)\n                cache[template_name] = template\n        # token.split_contents() isn't useful here because this tag doesn't accept\n    # variable as arguments.\n    bits = token.contents.split()\n    if len(bits) != 2:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' tag takes only one argument\" % bits[0])\n    block_name = bits[1]\n    # Keep track of the names of BlockNodes found in this template, so we can\n    # check for duplication.\n    try:\n        if block_name in parser.__loaded_blocks:\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n                \"'%s' tag with name '%s' appears more than once\" % (bits[0], block_name)\n            )\n        parser.__loaded_blocks.append(block_name)\n    except AttributeError:  # parser.__loaded_blocks isn't a list yet\n        parser.__loaded_blocks = [block_name]\n    nodelist = parser.parse((\"endblock\",))\n\n    # This check is kept for backwards-compatibility. See #3100.\n    endblock = parser.next_token()\n    acceptable_endblocks = (\"endblock\", \"endblock %s\" % block_name)\n    if endblock.contents not in acceptable_endblocks:\n    allow_recursion=False,\n):\n    \"\"\"\n    Convert a relative path (starting with './' or '../') to the full template\n    name based on the current_template_name.\n    \"\"\"\n    new_name = relative_name.strip(\"'\\\"\")\n    if not new_name.startswith((\"./\", \"../\")):\n        # relative_name is a variable or a literal that doesn't contain a\n        # relative path.\n        return relative_name\n\n    if current_template_name is None:\n        # Unknown origin (e.g. Template('...').render(Context({...})).\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            f\"The relative path {relative_name} cannot be evaluated due to \"\n            \"an unknown template origin.\"\n        )\n\n    new_name = posixpath.normpath(\n        posixpath.join(\n            posixpath.dirname(current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\")),\n            new_name,\n        )\n    )\n    if new_name.startswith(\"../\"):\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"The relative path '%s' points outside the file hierarchy that \"\n            \"template '%s' is in.\" % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    if not allow_recursion and current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\") == new_name:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \"\n            \"same template in which the tag appears.\"\n            % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    has_quotes = (\n        relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\n    )\n    return f'\"{new_name}\"' if has_quotes else new_name\n\n\n@register.tag(\"extends\")\ndef do_extends(parser, token):\n    \"\"\"\n    Signal that this template extends a parent template.\n\n    This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \"base\" %}`` (with quotes)\n    uses the literal value \"base\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\n    or ``{% extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n    name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n    the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a Template object).\n    \"\"\"\n    bits = token.split_contents()\n    if len(bits) != 2:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' takes one argument\" % bits[0])\n    bits[1] = construct_relative_path(parser.origin.template_name, bits[1])\n    parent_name = parser.compile_filter(bits[1])\n    nodelist = parser.parse()\n    if nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(ExtendsNode):\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"'%s' cannot appear more than once in the same template\" % bits[0]\n        )\n    return ExtendsNode(nodelist, parent_name)\n\n\n@register.tag(\"include\")\ndef do_include(parser, token):\n    \"\"\"\n    Load a template and render it with the current context. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-haiku-4-5-20251001","id":"msg_01DpPNHfSJvu9WtyUT7eyQop","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"thinking","thinking":"OK, I reverted that change. Let me think about this from first principles again.\n\nThe issue is: when rendering nested blocks at 3+ levels, the middle template's content leaks through instead of the innermost template's content.\n\nLet me think about what could cause the middle template's content to be rendered:\n1. The wrong block is being popped from the context\n2. The wrong block instance is being rendered\n3. The wrong nodelist is being used\n\nAll of these trace back to the block_context management.\n\nActually, let me re-examine the render() method in BlockNode one more time:\n\n```python\ndef render(self, context):\n    block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)    with context.push():        if block_context is None:\n            context[\"block\"] = self            result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n        else:\n            push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n            if block is None:\n                block = self            # Create new block so we can store context without            # thread-safety issues.\n            block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)            block.context = context            context[\"block\"] = block            result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n            if push is not None:\n                block_context.push(self.name, push)\n    return result\n```Wait! I just noticed something. Look at this code:```python\npush = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\nif block is None:\n    block = self# Create new block...\nblock = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n```\n\nThe line `block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)` is ALWAYS executed, even if `block is None`.If block is None (we didn't pop anything), then:- block = self (from the if statement)- block = type(self)(self.name, self.nodelist) (create a new block from self)So we create a new block from self.If block is not None (we popped something), then:\n\n\n- block = the popped block\n- block = type(self)(popped_block.name, popped_block.nodelist) (create a new block from the popped block)\n\nAh, there's the issue! When we instantiate the new block with `type(self)`, we're using the current instance's class, not the popped block's class. So if the current BlockNode is from the middle template, we're creating a new instance of that same type, even though we're using the popped block's name and nodelist. This could cause the wrong template content to render when we have deeply nested blocks.\n\nLooking at the actual code flow, the comment about thread-safety suggests we should always be creating a fresh block instance to avoid sharing state. But the current logic only creates one when the pop returns None, which seems backwards—we should be creating a new block from the popped result to isolate its context. The popped block's nodelist gets used to create the new instance—either self.nodelist if nothing was popped, or the retrieved block's nodelist if something was. The push logic also checks correctly: if pop() returns None, we skip the push back entirely. Let me examine the actual code flow once more to spot what might be going wrong.\n\nLooking at add_blocks() again, it inserts each block at position 0 in the context. I'm wondering if there's an issue when the same BlockNode instance appears in multiple places—like nested inside another block while also being added separately. If that instance gets rendered multiple times, it could affect internal state. Though actually, render() creates a fresh instance each time, so that shouldn't be the problem either.\n\nThe real issue might be more subtle. When a nested BlockNode from one template gets rendered as part of a parent block's content, and then we pop that block from the context by name, we're retrieving whatever instance is currently at the top of the stack for that name. This should work correctly regardless of which instance initiated the pop. But maybe the order in which blocks are added to the context is causing them to stack in an unexpected way. Let me explore whether the insertion order at position 0 is creating a precedence problem that affects which block gets selected during rendering.","signature":""}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":0,"cache_creation_input_tokens":698,"cache_read_input_tokens":90529,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":0,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":698},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"5363e7cb-e5dd-47f7-a7a1-25f2b067b5b3","uuid":"88a15fcb-b130-4b12-95d6-f5796b6f2925"}

Specifically, when we have 3 levels of inheritance with nested blocks, the current code might not properly handle the precedence of overrides.\n\nLet me look at the ExtendsNode.render() method one more time and think about the exact fix needed.The current order is:\n1. Add current's blocks (child's blocks)\n2. Check if parent is root, add parent's blocks (if root)\n3. Render parent\n\nBut when the parent is NOT a root (it also extends), the parent's ExtendsNode will be called during the rendering, which will add its blocks.\n\nSo the blocks are added in this order:\n1. Child's blocks\n2. Parent's blocks (added by parent's ExtendsNode)\n3. Grandparent's blocks (if grandparent is root, added by parent's ExtendsNode)\n\nThis seems correct. I just realized that when we call add_blocks() on the parent's blocks (line 154), we're adding ALL blocks found in the parent by get_nodes_by_type().\n\nBut get_nodes_by_type() finds ALL blocks, including NESTED ones!\n\nSo if the parent (base.html) has nested blocks, they would be added. But base.html doesn't have nested blocks in our test case.\n\nBut wait, what if there's an issue with how nested blocks are handled in middle.html?\n\nWhen middle.html is parsed, it has a nested \"inner\" block inside \"outer\". When we collect blocks from middle.html, we get both \"outer\" and \"inner\".\n\nWhen we add these to the block_context:\n- blocks[\"outer\"] = [middle_outer]\n\n\n- blocks[\"inner\"] = [middle_inner, child_inner]\n\nSo the \"inner\" queue should be [middle_inner, child_inner], with child_inner at the end. The issue might be in how we're collecting blocks from middle.html—perhaps we're inadvertently picking up blocks from elsewhere. Or when we pop and push blocks back, we're not properly restoring the state. Dictionary of FIFO queues.\n        self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n\n    def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n        for name, block in blocks.items():\n            self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n\n    def pop(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name].pop()\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n    def push(self, name, block):\n        self.blocks[name].append(block)\n\n    def get_block(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name][-1]\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n\nclass BlockNode(Node):\n    def __init__(self, name, nodelist, parent=None):\n        self.name = name\n        self.nodelist = nodelist\n        self.parent = parent\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return \"<Block Node: %s. Contents: %r>\" % (self.name, self.nodelist)\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\n        with context.push():\n            if block_context is None:\n                context[\"block\"] = self\n                result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n            else:\n                push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n                if block is None:\n                    block = self\n                # Create new block so we can store context without\n                # thread-safety issues.\n                block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n                block.context = context\n                context[\"block\"] = block\n                result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n                if push is not None:\n                    block_context.push(self.name, push)\n        return result\n\n    def super(self):\n        if not hasattr(self, \"context\"):\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n                \"'%s' object has no attribute 'context'. A history is kept in\n        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n        without extending the same template twice.\n        \"\"\"\n        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n            self.context_key,\n            [self.origin],\n        )\n        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n            template_name,\n            skip=history,\n        )\n        history.append(origin)\n        return template\n\n    def get_parent(self, context):\n        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n        if not parent:\n            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n                error_msg += (\n                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n                )\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n            # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\n        block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n\n        # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\n        # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n        for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n            # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\n            if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n                if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n                    blocks = {\n                        n.name: n\n                        for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n                    }\n                    block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n                break\n\n        # Call Template._render explicitly so the parser context stays\n        # the same.\n        with context.render_context.push_state(compiled_parent, isolated_context=False):\n            return compiled_parent._render(context)\n\n\nclass IncludeNode(Node):\n    context_key = \"__include_context\"\n\n    def __init__(\n        self, template, *args, extra_context=None, isolated_context=False, **kwargs\n    ):\n        self.template = template\n        self.extra_context = extra_context or {}\n        self.isolated_context = isolated_context\n        super().__init__(*args, **kwargs)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: template={self.template!r}>\"\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        \"\"\"\n        Render the specified template and context. Cache the template object\n        in render_context to avoid reparsing and loading when used in a for\n        loop.\n        \"\"\"\n        template = self.template.resolve(context)\n        # Does this quack like a Template?\n        if not callable(getattr(template, \"render\", None)):\n            # token.split_contents() isn't useful here because this tag doesn't accept\n    # variable as arguments.\n    bits = token.contents.split()\n    if len(bits) != 2:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' tag takes only one argument\" % bits[0])\n    block_name = bits[1]\n    # Keep track of the names of BlockNodes found in this template, so we can\n    # check for duplication.\n    try:\n        if block_name in parser.__loaded_blocks:\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n                \"'%s' tag with name '%s' appears more than once\" % (bits[0], block_name)\n            )\n        parser.__loaded_blocks.append(block_name)\n    except AttributeError:  # relative_name\n\n    if current_template_name is None:\n        # Unknown origin (e.g. Template('...').render(Context({...})).\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            f\"The relative path A history is kept in\n        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n        without extending the same template twice.\n        \"\"\"\n        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n            self.context_key,\n            [self.origin],\n        )\n        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n            template_name,\n            skip=history,\n        )\n        history.append(origin)\n        return template\n\n    def get_parent(self, context):\n        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n        if not parent:\n            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n                error_msg += (\n                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n                )\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n            # Cache the template object\n        in render_context to avoid reparsing and loading when used in a for\n        loop.\n        \"\"\"\n        template = self.template.resolve(context)\n        # Does this quack like a Template?\n        if not callable(getattr(template, \"render\", None)):\n            # token.split_contents() isn't useful here because this tag doesn't accept\n    # variable as arguments.\n    bits = token.contents.split()\n    if len(bits) != 2:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' tag takes only one argument\" % bits[0])\n    block_name = bits[1]\n    #    allow_recursion=False,\n):\n    \"\"\"\n    Convert a relative path (starting with './' or '../') to the full template\n    name based on the current_template_name.\n    \"\"\"\n    new_name = relative_name.strip(\"'\\\"\")\n    if not new_name.startswith((\"./\", \"../\")):\n        # relative_name is a variable or a literal that doesn't contain a\n        # relative path.\n        return relative_name\n\n    if current_template_name is None:\n        # Unknown origin (e.g. Template('...').render(Context({...})).\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            f\"The relative path {relative_name} cannot be evaluated due to \"\n            \"an unknown template origin.\"\n        )\n\n    new_name = posixpath.normpath(\n        posixpath.join(\n path '%s' points outside the file hierarchy that \"\n            \"template '%s' is in.\" % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    if not allow_recursion and current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\") == new_name:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \"\n            \"same template in which the tag appears.\"\n            % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    has_quotes = (\n        relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\n    )\n    return f'\"{new_name}\"' if has_quotes else new_name\n\n\n@register.tag(\"extends\")\ndef do_extends(parser, token):\n    \"\"\"\n    Signal that this template extends a parent template.\n\n    This tag may be used in two ways: ``{% extends \"base\" %}`` (with quotes)\n    uses the literal value \"base\" as the name of the parent template to extend,\n    or ``{% extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n    name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n    the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a Template object).\n    \"\"\"\n    bits = token.split_contents()\n    if len(bits) != 2:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\"'%s' takes one argument\" % bits[0])\n    bits[1] = construct_relative_path(parser.origin.template_name, bits[1])\n    parent_name = parser.compile_filter(bits[1])\n    nodelist = parser.parse()\n    if nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(ExtendsNode):\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"'%s' cannot appear more than once in the same template\" % bits[0]\n        )\n    return ExtendsNode(nodelist, parent_name)\n\n\n@register.tag(\"include\")\ndef do_include(parser, token):\n    \"\"\"\n    Load a template and render it with the current context. You can pass\n    additional context using keyword arguments.\n\n    Example::\n\n        {% include \"foo/some_include\" %}\n        {% include \"foo/some_include\" with bar=\"BAZZ!\" baz=\"BING!\" %}\n\n    Use the ``only`` argument to exclude the current context when rendering\n    the included template::\n\n        {% include \"foo/some_include\" only %}\n        {% include \"foo/some_include\" with bar=\"1\" only %}\n    \"\"\"\n    bits = token.split_contents()\n    if len(bits) < 2:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"%r tag takes at least one argument: the name of the template to \"\n            \"be included.\" % bits[0]\n        )\n    options = {}\n    remaining_bits = bits[2:]\n    while remaining_bits:\n        option = remaining_bits.pop(0)\n When we add blocks, we use insert(0, block), which inserts at the BEGINNING of the list. And when we pop, we use list.pop(), which removes from the END.\n\nThis is a LIFO queue where:- insert(0, x) adds to the front\n- pop() removes from the end\n\nSo if we have:\n1. Add child: [child]\n2. Add middle: [middle, child]\n3. Add base: [base, middle, child]When we pop:\n- pop() returns child (from the end) ✓\n\nBut WAIT! Dictionary of FIFO queues.\n        self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n\n    def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n        for name, block in blocks.items():\n            self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n\n    def pop(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name].pop()\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n    def push(self, name, block):\n        self.blocks[name].append(block)\n\n    def get_block(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name][-1]\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n\nclass BlockNode(Node):\n    def __init__(self, name, nodelist, parent=None):\n        self.name = name\n        self.nodelist = nodelist\n        self.parent = parent\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return \"<Block Node: %s. Contents: %r>\" % (self.name, self.nodelist)\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\n        with context.push():\n            if block_context is None:\n                context[\"block\"] = self\n                result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n            else:\n                push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n                if block is None:\n                    block = self\n                # Create new block so we can store context without\n                # thread-safety issues.\n                block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n                block.context = context\n                context[\"block\"] = block\n                result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n                if push is not None:\n                    block_context.push(self.name, push)\n        return result\n\n    def super(self):\n        if not hasattr(self, \"context\"):\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n                \"'%s' object has no attribute 'context'. Did you use \"\n                \"{{ block.super }} in a base template?\" % self.__class__.__name__\n            )\n        render_context = self.context.render_context\n        if (\n            BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY in render_context\n            and render_context[BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY].get_block(self.name) is not None\n        ):\n            return mark_safe(self.render(self.context))\n        return \"\"\n\n\nclass ExtendsNode(Node):\n    must_be_first = True\n    context_key = \"extends_context\"\n\n    def __init__(self, nodelist, parent_name, template_dirs=None):\n        self.nodelist = nodelist\n        self.parent_name = parent_name\n        self.template_dirs = template_dirs\n        self.blocks = {n.name: n for n in nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)}\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return \"<%s: extends %s>\" % (self.__class__.__name__, self.parent_name.token)\n\n    def find_template(self, template_name, context):\n        \"\"\"\n        This is a wrapper around engine.find_template(). A history is kept in\n        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n        without extending the same template twice.\n        \"\"\"\n        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n            self.context_key,\n            [self.origin],\n        )\n        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n            template_name,\n            skip=history,\n        )\n        history.append(origin)\n        return template\n\n    def get_parent(self, context):\n        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n        if not parent:\n            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n                error_msg += (\n                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n                )\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n            # Add the block nodes from this node to the block context\n        block_context.add_blocks(self.blocks)\n\n        # If this block's parent doesn't have an extends node it is the root,\n        # and its block nodes also need to be added to the block context.\n        for node in compiled_parent.nodelist:\n            # The ExtendsNode has to be the first non-text node.\n            if not isinstance(node, TextNode):\n                if not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n                    blocks = {\n                        n.name: n\n                        for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n                    }\n                    block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n                break\n\n        # current_template_name.lstrip(\"/\") == new_name:\n        raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"The relative path '%s' was translated to template name '%s', the \"\n            \"same template in which the tag appears.\"\n            % (relative_name, current_template_name)\n        )\n    has_quotes = (\n        relative_name.startswith(('\"', \"'\")) and relative_name[0] == relative_name[-1]\n    )\n    return f'\"{new_name}\"' if has_quotes else new_name\n\n\n@register.tag(\"extends\")\ndef do_extends(parser, token):\n    \"\"\"\n    Signal that this template extends a extends variable %}`` uses the value of ``variable`` as either the\n    name of the parent template to extend (if it evaluates to a string) or as\n    the parent template itself (if it evaluates to a raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n            \"'%s' cannot appear more than once in the same template\" % bits[0]\n        )\n    return ExtendsNode(nodelist, Maybe the issue is that my understanding of the problem is incorrect.Let me re-read the issue: \"the content from the middle template \"leaks through\" — meaning the middle template's block content appears instead of the innermost template's override.\"So we're seeing the MIDDLE template's content when we should see the INNERMOST template's content.This would happen if the wrong block is being rendered. And the wrong block would be rendered if:\n1. The wrong block is popped from the context\n2. Look at the line:```python\nblock = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n```This ALWAYS creates a NEW block, regardless of whether we popped something or not. And the NEW block is created with:- block.name - the NAME of the popped block (or self if nothing was popped)\n- block.nodelist - the NODELIST of the popped block (or self if nothing was popped)\n\n\nSo we're creating a new block with the CORRECT name and nodelist! But wait, let me re-examine the IF logic:```python\npush = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\nif block is None:\n    block = self# Create new block...block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n```\n\nIf block is None (we didn't pop anything):\n1. block is None\n2. In the IF: block = self (the current BlockNode instance from the template)\n3. Create new block: block = type(self)(self.name, self.nodelist)\n\nIf block is not None (we popped something):\n1. block is the popped BlockNode\n2. Skip the IF\n3. Create new block: block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist) — using the POPPED block's name and nodelist\n\nAh! So in both cases, we're creating a new BlockNode instance with the correct name and nodelist. The key difference is which nodelist we're using — either the current template's nodelist or the popped block's nodelist.\n\nNow I'm wondering if `type(self)` could be causing issues with subclasses, but looking at the code, BlockNode is the only class defined, so that shouldn't matter. After deep analysis, I think the real issue might be that when we add blocks from the parent template (when it's a root), those blocks should NOT include nested blocks that are already being managed by their parent blocks.\n\nBut actually, wait. If base.html doesn't have nested blocks, then this isn't an issue.\n\nLet me re-examine the exact scenario described in the issue one more time and think about what SPECIFICALLY could cause the middle template's content to leak through instead of the innermost template's content.\n\nThe only way I can see this happening is if:\n1. We pop the WRONG block from the context (middle instead of innermost)\n2. OR we render the wrong block instance\n3. OR we render the correct block but its nodelist is wrong\n\nAll of these should be prevented by the current logic!UNLESS... what if the issue is that when we add blocks from the PARENT (which is root), we're adding blocks that SHOULDN'T be added?\n\nLooking at the code:\n```python\nif not isinstance(node, ExtendsNode):\n    blocks = {        n.name: n\n        for n in compiled_parent.nodelist.get_nodes_by_type(BlockNode)\n    }\n    block_context.add_blocks(blocks)\n```\n\nThis adds ALL blocks from the parent's nodelist. But what if the parent's blocks shouldn't be added at this level?\n\nActually, they SHOULD be added, because they're the base definitions that will be overridden.\n\nWAIT! I just had a realization! What if the issue is that we're adding the parent's blocks AFTER the current node's blocks, which means they'll end up at a DIFFERENT position in the queue than expected?\n\nLet me trace through the queue states again, paying VERY close attention to the insert order:\n\nInitial: blocks = {}1. child.ExtendsNode adds {inner: child_inner}:   blocks[\"inner\"].insert(0, child_inner) -> [child_inner]2. \n\nWhen middle.ExtendsNode processes, it adds its own blocks {outer: middle_outer, inner: middle_inner} by inserting at position 0, so outer becomes [middle_outer] and inner becomes [middle_inner, child_inner]. Then when the parent's blocks are added, outer gets [base_outer, middle_outer]. When popping from the end, outer correctly yields middle_outer, but inner yields child_inner instead of middle_inner—this is the bug! The insertion order means the middle template's block gets buried beneath the child's block in the queue. Let me trace through the rendering to see if nested blocks are being processed differently than expected. Dictionary of FIFO queues.\n        self.blocks = defaultdict(list)\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return f\"<{self.__class__.__qualname__}: blocks={self.blocks!r}>\"\n\n    def add_blocks(self, blocks):\n        for name, block in blocks.items():\n            self.blocks[name].insert(0, block)\n\n    def pop(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name].pop()\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n    def push(self, name, block):\n        self.blocks[name].append(block)\n\n    def get_block(self, name):\n        try:\n            return self.blocks[name][-1]\n        except IndexError:\n            return None\n\n\nclass BlockNode(Node):\n    def __init__(self, name, nodelist, parent=None):\n        self.name = name\n        self.nodelist = nodelist\n        self.parent = parent\n\n    def __repr__(self):\n        return \"<Block Node: %s. Contents: %r>\" % (self.name, self.nodelist)\n\n    def render(self, context):\n        block_context = context.render_context.get(BLOCK_CONTEXT_KEY)\n        with context.push():\n            if block_context is None:\n                context[\"block\"] = self\n                result = self.nodelist.render(context)\n            else:\n                push = block = block_context.pop(self.name)\n                if block is None:\n                    block = self\n                # Create new block so we can store context without\n                # thread-safety issues.\n                block = type(self)(block.name, block.nodelist)\n                block.context = context\n                context[\"block\"] = block\n                result = block.nodelist.render(context)\n                if push is not None:\n                    block_context.push(self.name, push)\n        return result\n\n    def super(self):\n        if not hasattr(self, \"context\"):\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(\n                \"'%s' object has no attribute 'context'. A history is kept in\n        the render_context attribute between successive extends calls and\n        passed as the skip argument. This enables extends to work recursively\n        without extending the same template twice.\n        \"\"\"\n        history = context.render_context.setdefault(\n            self.context_key,\n            [self.origin],\n        )\n        template, origin = context.template.engine.find_template(\n            template_name,\n            skip=history,\n        )\n        history.append(origin)\n        return template\n\n    def get_parent(self, context):\n        parent = self.parent_name.resolve(context)\n        if not parent:\n            error_msg = \"Invalid template name in 'extends' tag: %r.\" % parent\n            if self.parent_name.filters or isinstance(self.parent_name.var, Variable):\n                error_msg += (\n                    \" Got this from the '%s' variable.\" % self.parent_name.token\n                )\n            raise TemplateSyntaxError(error_msg)\n        if isinstance(parent, Template):\n            #
